content:
This article will provide a comprehensive comparison between Compass vs Keller Williams to help you determine which brokerage better aligns with your professional goals. Choosing the right brokerage is a critical decision for any real estate agent, as it significantly impacts their career trajectory and financial success.

Commission Splits and Fees

The commission splits and associated fees are crucial factors to consider when selecting a brokerage. Keller Williams offers flexible splits ranging from 70/30 to 100/0, with a 6% franchise/royalty fee until the cap is reached. Compass provides splits starting at 60/40 and going up to 92.5/7.5, with no franchise/royalty fee, and only some agents get a production cap.

Monthly fees for Keller Williams vary by office, whereas Compass charges $145 and varies by office. Additionally, Keller Williams does not charge transaction fees, while Compass charges up to 4% per transaction. Keller Williams has a more straightforward fee structure, potentially making it more predictable for agents.

Income Opportunities

The potential for additional income streams can significantly enhance an agent’s financial stability and growth. Keller Williams stands out by offering revenue share programs that provide a retirement and willable income path. These additional income streams can offer substantial long-term financial benefits and security. Conversely, Compass does not offer such supplementary income opportunities, limiting agents primarily to their commission-based earnings.

Training and Support

Effective training and support are vital for both new and experienced agents. Keller Williams excels in this area, providing extensive training opportunities, though specific details on the extent of their offerings are not standardized across all offices. Agents at Keller Williams also benefit from various support structures, although 24/7 support is not available. In contrast, Compass offers training through Compass Academy, but similar to Keller Williams, 24/7 support is not provided. This difference can be crucial for agents who require ongoing assistance and professional development.

Technology and Resources

In the modern real estate landscape, leveraging technology effectively is essential. Keller Williams is known for its robust technology platforms, offering tools that enhance productivity and client engagement. On the other hand, Compass is also recognized for its technological advancements, including its Compass Academy for training. Both brokerages provide valuable technological resources, but the specific tools and platforms offered may appeal differently depending on the agent’s needs.

Agent Satisfaction

Agent satisfaction is a key indicator of a brokerage’s effectiveness and overall agent happiness. Keller Williams boasts a high Glassdoor rating of 4.3 stars based on 7.9K reviews, indicating strong agent approval and satisfaction. Compass holds a lower rating of 3.7 stars from 2.4K reviews, suggesting that there may be areas needing improvement. The differences in agent satisfaction can often be attributed to the varying levels of support, training, and income opportunities provided by each brokerage.

Final Thoughts on Compass vs Keller Williams

In comparing Compass and Keller Williams, it’s evident that Keller Williams offers more favorable commission splits, extensive training, and additional income opportunities, making it a compelling choice for both new and experienced agents. The innovative technology and comprehensive support further enhance Keller Williams’ appeal. Compass, while also offering advanced technology and structured training through Compass Academy, has higher fees and fewer income paths, which may limit its appeal to agents seeking comprehensive support and modern business solutions.

For a more detailed comparison and to find the best brokerage for your needs, check out our brokerage comparison tool.

When making your decision, consider your specific needs and career objectives. Evaluate the advantages and disadvantages carefully to choose the brokerage that best fits your professional aspirations. Whether you prioritize comprehensive support and training or seek a brokerage with a more conventional setup, your decision should align with your long-term career goals and preferences.

title: eXp Realty vs Real Brokerage: An Honest Comparison for Agents in 2026
excerpt: eXp Realty vs Real Financial Strength Agent Earnings Agent Fees Support Cloud Based Brokerage eXp Realty Real Broker Founded 2009 2014 GlassDoor Rating X/5 4.6…
content:
The Real Brokerage has been one of the fastest-growing cloud brokerages in North America, attracting agents with a competitive commission split, AI-powered tools, and a revenue share program that pays out 60% of monthly company revenue. If you’re comparing Real Brokerage to eXp Realty, you’re evaluating two agent-centric, cloud-first models that both promise passive income and equity ownership. The differences are in the details – and those details matter when you’re choosing where to build your career.

Both brokerages operate without physical offices, both offer revenue share programs, and both provide stock incentives. But eXp Realty has over 90,000 agents worldwide, a publicly traded stock on the Nasdaq (EXPI), and has paid out more than $889 million in revenue share. Real Brokerage (REAX) is publicly traded as well but is a newer company with a smaller agent base that’s still scaling its programs. The question comes down to which platform delivers more total value over the long run – not just which one costs less on paper.

This comparison breaks down every major category using verified 2026 data so you can see exactly how these two cloud brokerages stack up. We’ll cover commission structures, total annual costs, revenue share programs, training, technology, culture, and equity opportunities.

What We’ll Cover

  • Commission Structure
  • Total Annual Cost to the Agent
  • Revenue Share vs Revenue Share
  • Training and Education
  • Technology and Tools
  • Culture and Community
  • Stock and Equity Opportunities
  • Who Should Choose eXp Realty
  • Who Should Choose Real Brokerage
  • The Bottom Line
  • Frequently Asked Questions

Commission Structure

Real Brokerage uses a commission model that starts at 85/15 and transitions to 100/0 after cap. The structure is straightforward, but the fees that layer on top – especially after cap – add up in ways that aren’t immediately obvious.

Real Brokerage Commission Structure

  • 85/15 commission split with a $12,000 annual cap ($6,000 for teams, $4,000 for mega teams)
  • After cap: 100% commission for the remainder of the anniversary year
  • Post-cap transaction fee: $285 per sale ($129 for Elite Agents)
  • $750/year annual fee ($250 deducted from each of your first 3 transactions)
  • $249 one-time startup fee for new agents
  • $40/transaction CBR fee (E&O insurance equivalent)
  • 0% franchise or royalty fee

eXp Realty Commission Structure

eXp Realty uses a single, standardized commission plan for all agents:

  • 80/20 commission split with a $16,000 annual cap
  • After cap: 100% commission for the remainder of the anniversary year
  • $250 per-transaction fee after cap (max 20 transactions), then $75 sponsor fee per transaction
  • $85/month technology fee ($175/month for first 6 months for new agents)
  • Approximately $40-65/month for E&O insurance
  • 0% franchise or royalty fee

Real’s 85/15 split means you keep more per transaction before cap ($12K vs $16K), which is appealing. But the post-cap fees, per-transaction CBR charges, and annual fee structure add costs that narrow the gap. And eXp’s ICON Agent program can rebate the entire $16,000 cap as publicly traded stock – something Real doesn’t match.

Total Annual Cost to the Agent

Here’s how the numbers break down for an agent producing $250,000 in GCI across 20 transactions in a year.

Real Brokerage at $250K GCI

Fee CategoryAmount
Commission to Real (15% until $12K cap)$12,000
Annual Fee$750
CBR Fee / E&O (20 x $40)$800
Post-Cap Transaction Fees (~12 sales x $285)$3,420
Total Annual Cost$16,970

Note: Cap is reached after approximately $80,000 in GCI (15% x $80K = $12K). The remaining ~12 transactions are post-cap and incur the $285/sale fee. Elite Agents pay $129/sale post-cap, reducing total to approximately $15,298.

eXp Realty at $250K GCI

Fee CategoryAmount
Commission to eXp (20% until $16K cap)$16,000
Monthly Tech Fee ($85 x 12)$1,020
E&O Insurance (~$50/mo)$600
Total Annual Cost$17,620

The Comparison

Real Brokerage saves you approximately $650 per year in direct costs at $250K GCI. For Elite Agents at Real, the savings increase to roughly $2,322. The gap is relatively small for standard agents – close enough that the decision should be driven by the value of each platform’s tools, stock programs, and revenue share rather than pure cost.

And once again, eXp’s ICON Agent program changes the math entirely. An ICON-qualifying agent gets their full $16,000 cap rebated as EXPI stock, reducing their effective cost to just $1,620 (tech fee + E&O). That makes eXp dramatically cheaper than Real for top producers who meet the benchmarks.

Revenue Share vs Revenue Share

Both eXp and Real offer revenue share programs, making this one of the most direct comparisons in the cloud brokerage space. Both are designed to reward agents for growing the company. The structural differences determine which one builds more long-term wealth.

eXp Realty Revenue Share

eXp’s revenue share program distributes a percentage of the company dollar (the brokerage’s portion of each agent’s commission) across seven tiers of sponsorship:

TierPercentage of Company DollarMax Agents in Tier
Tier 1 (direct sponsors)50% (3.5% of GCI)Unlimited
Tier 210% (0.7% of GCI)Unlimited
Tier 35% (0.35% of GCI)Unlimited
Tier 45% (0.35% of GCI)Unlimited
Tier 55% (0.35% of GCI)Unlimited
Tier 62.5% (0.175% of GCI)Unlimited
Tier 72.5% (0.175% of GCI)Unlimited

eXp has paid out over $889 million in revenue share since the program’s inception. The program is fully willable from day one and can be passed to heirs, creating a genuine retirement income path with no vesting period.

Real Brokerage Revenue Share

Real’s revenue share program uses a 5-tier structure and distributes 60% of monthly company revenue to the agent network:

TierPercentage
Tier 1 (direct attracts)5%
Tier 24%
Tier 33%
Tier 42%
Tier 51%

Real’s program requires 3 consecutive producing years before revenue share is 100% vested. Once vested, it becomes fully willable.

Key Differences

Depth of tiers: eXp pays across 7 tiers, meaning you earn from agents further down your sponsorship tree. Real pays across 5 tiers. For agents building large organizations, those extra two tiers compound significantly over time – every agent in tiers 6 and 7 generates income at eXp that simply doesn’t exist at Real.

Scale and track record: eXp’s $889M+ in total payouts across 90,000+ agents is a verified, public number backed by over a decade of operation. Real’s program is newer and operating at smaller scale. More agents in the network means more company dollars being generated, which means larger payouts at every tier.

Willability and vesting: eXp’s revenue share is fully willable from day one – no waiting period, no conditions. Real requires 3 consecutive producing years before the income becomes fully vested and willable. If you leave Real or stop producing before the 3-year mark, your revenue share organization may not transfer to your heirs.

Distribution model: Real distributes 60% of monthly company revenue, which sounds generous. But the 5-tier structure with lower percentages (5%/4%/3%/2%/1%) limits how much flows to each sponsor. eXp’s Tier 1 at 50% of the company dollar is notably higher than Real’s 5% of revenue, though the calculation bases are different.

Training and Education

Real Brokerage Training

Real Brokerage has invested in building out its training infrastructure:

  • Real Academy: 30+ live training sessions per week covering sales, marketing, and business building
  • Agent BreakThru: Free 8-week coaching program for agents looking to level up their business
  • On-demand content: Recorded sessions and training materials accessible anytime
  • Leo AI concierge: AI-powered assistant available 24/7 for quick answers and support

The Agent BreakThru coaching program is a standout offering – a structured 8-week program at no additional cost. For agents who prefer intensive, time-bound coaching over ongoing self-paced learning, this format can drive faster results.

eXp Realty Training

eXp Realty operates one of the most comprehensive training platforms in the industry:

  • eXp University: 50+ hours of live training per week across every specialization
  • eXp Collaborate: Company-wide collaboration platform connecting 90,000+ agents for real-time support
  • Mentorship program: New agents are paired with experienced mentors for their first transactions
  • On-demand library: Thousands of recorded sessions searchable by topic
  • ICON Agent mentorship: Top producers share strategies through dedicated training tracks

Both brokerages take training seriously. The scale difference is the main factor – eXp’s 90,000+ agent network means more specialized training tracks, more niche expertise available, and a deeper bench of experienced agents to learn from. Real’s 30+ sessions per week is solid, but eXp’s 50+ hours per week with specialists in luxury, commercial, investment, new construction, and dozens of other niches provides broader coverage.

Technology and Tools

Real Brokerage Technology

Real has leaned into technology as a core differentiator:

  • Leo AI: An AI-powered concierge available 24/7 that can answer questions, assist with transactions, and provide support
  • Real mobile app: Purpose-built app for managing your business on the go
  • Transaction management: Built-in tools for managing deals from contract to close
  • Cloud-based operations: No physical office requirements

Leo AI is Real’s flagship tech feature – an AI concierge that aims to provide instant support and reduce the need for traditional broker assistance. It’s an innovative approach that reflects where the industry is heading.

eXp Realty Technology

eXp includes enterprise-grade technology as part of the standard $85/month tech fee:

  • kvCORE: Full CRM with IDX website, lead generation, automated follow-up, and transaction management (valued at $500+/month as a standalone product)
  • eXp Collaborate: Company-wide communication and collaboration platform
  • Skyslope: Transaction management and digital signatures
  • Custom agent website: IDX-enabled personal website included
  • Marketing center: Customizable marketing materials and templates

The key difference is comprehensiveness. eXp’s kvCORE platform is a full enterprise CRM that handles lead generation, automated follow-up sequences, IDX website, and transaction management in one system. It’s valued at $500+/month as a standalone product and comes included with eXp’s tech fee. Real’s tech stack is solid and the Leo AI concierge is innovative, but agents may still need to source their own CRM and lead generation tools separately.

Culture and Community

Real Brokerage Culture

Real Brokerage has built a culture centered on:

  • Tech-forward, innovation-driven mindset
  • Agent-first philosophy with transparent communication
  • Growing community of agents who value efficiency and modern tools
  • Active social media presence and online community engagement

Real attracts agents who identify with the “tech startup” energy – a fast-moving company that’s willing to innovate and disrupt. The culture appeals to agents who see themselves as entrepreneurs building alongside a growing company.

eXp Realty Culture

eXp Realty has built one of the most distinctive cultures in real estate:

  • eXp Collaborate: Always-on platform connecting agents globally for instant support and collaboration
  • EXPCON: Annual conference bringing thousands of agents together for training and networking
  • Revenue share alignment: Sponsors earn when their recruits succeed, creating genuine incentives to help others grow
  • Agent-owned mentality: Stock ownership gives agents a real stake in the company’s performance

Both brokerages share the “agents as stakeholders” philosophy through stock and revenue share. eXp’s advantage is time and scale – with over a decade of culture-building and 90,000+ agents, the collaborative ecosystem is deeper, more diverse, and more established. Real’s culture is still forming, which is either a limitation or an opportunity depending on your perspective.

Stock and Equity Opportunities

This is one area where eXp and Real are more similar than different – both are publicly traded companies that offer stock to agents. But the programs differ in structure, scale, and earning potential.

Real Brokerage Stock (REAX)

  • Top Agent Bonus: Up to $24,000 in RSUs ($16,000 for production milestones + $8,000 for cultural contributions)
  • RSU vesting: Stock awards vest over 3 years
  • Elite Agent Program: Top producers earn reduced post-cap fees ($129 vs $285) and additional stock incentives
  • Publicly traded: REAX trades on public markets, providing liquidity

eXp Realty Stock (EXPI)

  • ICON Agent Award: Earn back your entire $16,000 cap as EXPI stock by meeting production and cultural benchmarks
  • Agent Equity Program: Purchase EXPI stock at a 10% discount through payroll deduction
  • Sustainable Equity Plan: Receive stock grants at key milestones (first transaction, annual anniversary, capping)
  • Revenue share in stock: Option to receive revenue share payments as EXPI stock at a 5% bonus

Key Differences

Vesting vs immediate: Real’s $24K top agent bonus is paid in RSUs that vest over 3 years. eXp’s ICON award of $16K in stock is earned within the anniversary year. Both require you to stay and produce, but eXp’s stock is available sooner.

Multiple earning paths: eXp offers four distinct ways to earn stock (ICON, equity program, milestone grants, revenue share conversion). Real’s stock program is primarily through the top agent bonus and Elite status. eXp’s approach lets agents at every production level participate in equity building, not just top producers.

Market capitalization: EXPI has a significantly larger market cap than REAX, which generally means more trading volume, more analyst coverage, and more liquidity. Both stocks can be bought and sold on public markets, but higher volume makes it easier to trade without impacting the price.

Compounding effect: eXp agents can convert revenue share payments to stock at a 5% bonus, creating a compounding loop where passive income builds equity which builds more value over time. Real doesn’t offer an equivalent conversion mechanism.

Who Should Choose eXp Realty

eXp Realty is the stronger choice if you:

  • Want the most proven revenue share program: $889M+ paid out across 90,000+ agents with 7 tiers and immediate willability from day one
  • Are targeting ICON status: Getting your $16,000 cap rebated as stock makes eXp cheaper than Real while also building equity
  • Need an enterprise CRM included: kvCORE with IDX website, lead generation, and automated follow-up comes standard
  • Want deeper training coverage: 50+ hours weekly with specialists in every niche, plus a formal mentorship program
  • Value immediate willability: No vesting period – your revenue share organization is willable from day one
  • Prefer more ways to earn stock: Four distinct equity programs vs Real’s primarily top-agent-focused approach

Who Should Choose Real Brokerage

Real Brokerage could be a fit if you:

  • Want slightly lower direct costs: The 85/15 split with a $12K cap saves roughly $650/year vs eXp at $250K GCI
  • Are drawn to AI-powered tools: Leo AI concierge is an innovative approach to 24/7 agent support that may appeal to tech-savvy agents
  • Prefer a smaller, high-growth environment: Real’s smaller agent base means more early-mover opportunities in revenue share
  • Like the top agent bonus structure: The $24K RSU bonus ($16K production + $8K cultural) rewards both sales and community building
  • Want lower team caps: $6K team cap and $4K mega team cap are attractive for team leaders
  • Value the structured coaching format: The free 8-week Agent BreakThru program provides intensive, time-bound coaching

The Bottom Line

Real Brokerage and eXp Realty are two of the most compelling cloud brokerage options available to agents in 2026. Both offer revenue share, stock incentives, and a cloud-first model. The direct cost difference at $250K GCI is only about $650 – close enough that it shouldn’t be the deciding factor.

Real’s strengths are its lower cap ($12K vs $16K), the Leo AI concierge, team-friendly pricing, and the $24K top agent RSU bonus. These are real advantages, especially for high-volume producers and team leaders who want lower overhead and cutting-edge AI tools.

eXp’s strengths compound over time. A 7-tier revenue share program with $889M+ in proven payouts vs Real’s 5-tier program. Immediate willability vs a 3-year vesting period. Four ways to earn publicly traded stock vs primarily one. An enterprise CRM (kvCORE) included in the tech fee. And the ICON program that can rebate your entire cap as stock, effectively making eXp cheaper than Real for agents who qualify.

The decision often comes down to time horizon. If you’re optimizing for the lowest possible cost today, Real’s numbers are attractive. If you’re building toward long-term wealth through revenue share compounding, stock accumulation, and ICON eligibility, eXp’s ecosystem is designed to reward that patience. Both are legitimate choices – the right one depends on what you’re building toward.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do eXp and Real Brokerage commission structures compare?

Real uses an 85/15 split with a $12,000 cap, while eXp uses an 80/20 split with a $16,000 cap. Real’s lower split percentage and lower cap mean you pay less to reach 100% commission. However, Real charges a $285 post-cap transaction fee ($129 for Elite Agents) and a $40 CBR fee per transaction that continue after cap. At $250K GCI, Real costs approximately $16,970 vs eXp’s $17,620 – a difference of only about $650.

Are both eXp and Real Brokerage publicly traded?

Yes. eXp Realty trades as EXPI on the Nasdaq, and Real Brokerage trades as REAX. Both stocks can be bought and sold on public markets. The key difference is that EXPI has a significantly larger market capitalization, which typically means more trading volume, more analyst coverage, and easier liquidity when you want to sell shares.

How do the revenue share programs compare?

eXp uses a 7-tier structure with Tier 1 paying 50% of the company dollar (3.5% of GCI). Real uses a 5-tier structure (5%/4%/3%/2%/1%) and distributes 60% of monthly company revenue. eXp has paid out $889M+ total and offers immediate willability. Real’s program requires 3 consecutive producing years before revenue share is fully vested and willable. The extra two tiers at eXp also mean you earn from agents deeper in your organization.

What is Real Brokerage’s Leo AI and does eXp have anything similar?

Leo is Real’s AI-powered concierge available 24/7 that can answer questions, provide transaction support, and assist with common agent needs. It’s one of Real’s most distinctive technology features. eXp doesn’t have a direct AI concierge equivalent, but provides 24/7 broker support, the eXp Collaborate platform for real-time help from 90,000+ agents, and the comprehensive kvCORE CRM suite for managing your business.

What is eXp’s ICON Agent program and how does it affect the cost comparison?

ICON is eXp’s top-producer recognition program. Agents who meet production benchmarks and cultural contributions receive their entire $16,000 cap back as EXPI stock. For an ICON agent, the effective annual cost drops to roughly $1,620 (tech fee + E&O) – far less than Real’s $16,970. You’re also accumulating $16,000 in publicly traded stock each year. Real’s top agent bonus maxes at $24,000 in RSUs but requires 3-year vesting and doesn’t reduce your commission costs.

Which brokerage is better for teams?

Real Brokerage has more attractive team pricing – $6,000 cap for teams and $4,000 for mega teams vs eXp’s standard $16,000 cap. This lower team cap makes Real particularly competitive for team leaders who want to minimize overhead for their members. eXp offers team structures as well, but the cap advantage clearly favors Real for team-oriented business models.

Is Real Brokerage revenue share willable like eXp’s?

Both programs are willable, but with different conditions. eXp’s revenue share is fully willable from day one – no vesting period, no conditions. Real’s revenue share requires 3 consecutive producing years before it’s 100% vested and willable. If you leave Real or stop producing before reaching the 3-year mark, your revenue share organization may not fully transfer to your heirs. For agents prioritizing legacy income, eXp’s immediate willability eliminates that risk.
